focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Dan was standing in the kitchen eating crackers over the sink. Not on a plate — just the box of Triscuits propped against his hip, crumbs dropping onto the stainless steel and into the drain where they'd stay until he remembered to wipe them or Mira did it first and said nothing about it, which was worse than saying something because saying nothing had become its own language in this apartment, a language of judgments too small to fight over and too constant to ignore. At 4:47 on a Thursday, his phone buzzed in his pocket. From the bedroom, through the door Mira left three-quarters open because closing it all the way would be a statement, he heard her phone vibrate against the nightstand — two devices pulsing at the same instant with the synchrony that only institutional systems achieved.

He pulled out his phone.

QUARTERLY DEVELOPMENTAL ASSESSMENT — STUDENT: THEODORE D. KESSLER — Q1 REVIEW CYCLE COMPLETE — FULL REPORT AVAILABLE — SHARED PARENTAL ACCESS GRANTED

He read it twice. The cracker in his mouth had gone to paste against the roof of his palate and he swallowed it dry.

Down the hall, in the bedroom that was technically theirs and practically Mira's, he heard her pick up the phone. A pause. Then the sound of her feet on the floor, the rhythm of Mira walking toward a problem rather than away from one — quick, even, her heels landing first.

She appeared in the kitchen doorway holding her phone at the angle people held phones when they were processing bad news — slightly away from the face, as though distance from the screen could dilute what was on it. She wore the old Wesleyan sweatshirt she put on at home, the one she'd stopped wearing in public around the time their marriage started classifying as infrastructure, and her feet were bare on the ki...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...s going to keep practicing. Mira asked if he'd finished his reading assignment. Theo said mostly. Dan chewed and listened and watched his son's hands moving as he talked, the small fingers that held pencils and chopsticks and the straps of his backpack, and for a moment his hand was back in Room 104, the pen, the scratch of ink on paper — and then Theo was telling him about a circle that looked like an egg and this was, for the moment, the whole world, and the whole world was enough, and he was going to let it be enough for as long as the evening lasted, which was not long, which was never long enough.

After Theo went to bed, Dan sat on the couch in the living room with his laptop open and a show playing that he wasn't watching. The apartment ticked and hummed around him. Mira was in the bedroom with the door ajar, reading something on her tablet, the blue light of the screen casting a faint glow that he could see from the hallway. He looked at his right hand. The ink was still there, in the lines of his palm, a trace of what he'd done. He could still feel the weight of the pen. He could still hear the sound it made on the paper — that scratching, that drag of ballpoint on a corporate document, the loudest sound in a room where a man agrees to something he can't name.

He closed the laptop. He sat in the dark. The building's sounds settled around him: a pipe knocking softly somewhere in the walls, the refrigerator's forty-minute click, the creak of a floorboard in Theo's room as the boy shifted in his sleep. Through the window, the city did what the city did — lights and motion and the distant wail of something that might have been a siren or might have been the wind through the gap between buildings, the sound of a world running itself.

Dan sat there for a long time.
